title: Why we love this country - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Tyler Cowen's Free Press 250th-anniversary entry lists roughly 20 disparate things he loves about America — from the world's best AI models and deepest capital markets to baseball's infield fly rule and cheeseburgers — refusing to pick one favorite, implicitly framing American strength as institutional and cultural breadth rather than any single achievement.

insights.claim: Tyler Cowen frames his admiration for the U.S. as a refusal to rank, listing roughly 20 items spanning technology, culture, and institutions instead of naming one favorite, treating breadth and pluralism rather than any single achievement as the mark of national strength.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Shows how a leading pro-growth economist frames national strength for a mainstream audience — as institutional and cultural depth rather than a single metric like GDP — relevant context for reading his broader commentary on U.S. competitiveness.
insights.quote: My favorite thing about America is that I do not have a single favorite thing.

insights.claim: Cowen's list places the world's best AI models and the deepest capital markets on the same footing as cultural minutiae like the infield fly rule and cheeseburgers, treating U.S. AI leadership and capital-market depth as uncontested, taken-for-granted national assets.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A signal that mainstream elite economic opinion still treats U.S. AI dominance and capital-market depth as globally unrivaled circa mid-2026, worth tracking against competing narratives of eroding U.S. tech/financial leadership.
insights.locator: We have the NBA (with a Toronto team too)

insights.claim: Cowen's parenthetical aside about America having 'three branches of government (I hope not four)' registers a veiled concern about executive or bureaucratic power expanding beyond the constitutional separation of powers.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A subtle signal of unease among a prominent center-right-leaning economist about institutional erosion, worth watching alongside his other commentary on executive power and regulatory state growth.
insights.quote: three branches of government (I hope not four)
insights.locator: not just one but two Dakotas

insights.claim: Cowen explicitly names immigrants as 'the best set of immigrants in the world,' an unqualified endorsement of the current immigrant population as a source of American advantage, consistent with his long-standing pro-immigration policy stance.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Reinforces his long-held view that immigration inflow is a top-tier structural advantage for the U.S. economy, relevant to forecasting his positioning on immigration-policy debates affecting labor supply and innovation.
insights.quote: the best set of immigrants in the world
